html:
COMMISSION IMPLEMENTING REGULATION (EU) 2026/1457

concerning the allocation of tariff quotas opened pursuant to Regulation (EU) 2026/1384 of the European Parliament and of the Council on measures to address the negative trade-related effects of global overcapacity on the Union's steel market, and amending Regulation (EU) 2020/2170

THE EUROPEAN COMMISSION HAS ADOPTED THIS REGULATION:

Having regard to the Treaty on the Functioning of the European Union, Having regard to Regulation (EU) 2026/1384 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 17 June 2026 on measures to address the negative trade-related effects of global overcapacity on the Union's steel market, and amending Regulation (EU) 2020/2170 (hereinafter referred to as "the steel regulation"), in particular Article 5.1 and 5.5, and Whereas, on 25 June 2026, the steel regulation entered into force, establishing tariff quotas of 18,345,922 tonnes for 26 product categories and setting an out-of-quota duty of 50% (the additional duty is specified in Annex 1).

The steel regulation grants the Commission the authority to adopt implementing acts to determine the allocation of tariff quotas by country, taking into account the Union's interest and, where applicable, tariff quota levels corresponding to the import market share that prevailed in the EU steel market in 2013; tariff quotas per product category based on the proportion of imports held by each product category during the period 2022-2024; existing and future free trade agreements; distorting effects on trade caused by measures taken by third countries that affect the EU steel market; whether a third country has been found to be in violation of ILO conventions or multilateral environmental agreements; international agreements concluded by the Union pursuant to Article XXVIII of the General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade (GATT 1994) relating to tariff quotas; international agreements or non-binding international arrangements dealing with levels of global overcapacity for the product categories covered by the steel regulation; diversification of sources of supply; the situation in a candidate country facing an exceptional and immediate security threat, especially if it has previously benefited from preferential access to the EU steel market.

The tariff quotas in Annex II to the steel regulation consist of two parts. One part is available to all third countries based on the principle of most-favored nation (the MFN portion), while the other part is only available to countries covered by an existing or future free trade agreement with the Union (the free trade agreement portion). For transparency reasons, the allocation between these two portions of the tariff quotas is specified in Annex I regarding "tariff quota volumes."

Regarding the MGN component, it is crucial to ensure that the allocation of tariff quotas aligns with the European Union's obligations under WTO law. Therefore, tariff quotas should be distributed to trading partners in a non-discriminatory manner (based on each partner's average share of EU imports in a specific category during the period 2022–2024), in accordance with Article XIII of GATT 1994.

When allocating tariff quotas under the free trade agreement component, it is appropriate to consider the following: existing or future free trade agreements, supplemented by any preliminary agreements concluded within negotiations conducted in accordance with Article XXVIII of GATT 1994; distorting effects on trade caused by measures taken by third countries that affect the EU's steel market; the need to ensure diversification of supply and the situation of a candidate country facing an exceptional and immediate security threat, especially if it has previously benefited from preferential access to the EU's steel market. Consequently, certain free trade agreement partners should have differentiated access to tariff quotas opened under the steel regulation. In particular, Ukraine should receive a more favorable allocation of tariff quotas than other free trade agreement partners.

It is necessary to establish the list of existing or future free trade agreements with the Union mentioned in Article 5.1 c of the steel regulation. The list should include all such agreements that have been signed or entered into force at the time this regulation enters into force, as well as any agreements for which a political agreement has been reached by the same date, and which cover any of the product categories listed in Annex I to the steel regulation.

Specific provisions for the management of tariff quotas should be established to ensure that economic actors have a clear understanding of the interaction between the different types of tariff quotas opened under the steel regulation and this regulation.

A country-specific quota should be allocated based on a specific country's share of imports per product category during the reference period (2022–2024). Countries with a significant import share within a particular product category during that period should receive a country-specific quota for that product category.

A country-specific quota will be allocated within the MGN portion, and when a country has an existing or future free trade agreement with the Union, economic actors will also have access to the free trade agreement portion of the country-specific quota. Both the MGN portion and the free trade agreement portion of the country-specific quota are available simultaneously to economic actors and function as a single quota with a single identification number. If a country is not listed in section 1 of Annex II, economic actors can only access the MGN portion of the country-specific quota.

When a country-specific quota opened for a country that has an existing or future free trade agreement with the Union is exhausted, operators from that country should also be given access to an additional tariff quota (free trade agreement quota – country-specific quota). This quota is available on a "first come, first served" basis and is accessible by economic actors from countries that have an existing or future free trade agreement with the Union after they have exhausted their respective country-specific quotas. Section 2 of Annex II specifies the origin of products eligible for the free trade agreement quota – country-specific quota in each product category.

When a country does not have a specific quota for a particular product category, that country should only be eligible for the remaining portion of the quota. This remaining portion is also divided into two parts: a remaining "Most Favored Nation" (MFN) portion and a remaining free trade agreement portion. The remaining MFN portion (listed in Appendix I under "Other Countries") is available to all countries, except those listed in section 3 of Appendix II. The remaining free trade agreement portion (listed in Appendix I under "Free Trade Agreement Quota - Other Countries") is only available to countries listed in section 1 of Appendix II. Section 4 of Appendix II specifies which countries have access to the Free Trade Agreement Quota for each product category.

Countries that have an existing or future free trade agreement with the Union, as listed in section 1 of Appendix II, should have access to both portions of the remaining quota mentioned in point 11, and these quotas should be made available simultaneously.
tldr: The European Union determines the amount of steel that can be imported from countries outside the EU without incurring additional tariffs. The purpose is to protect the European steel market while also regulating trade with other nations.
lattlast:
The EU has implemented regulations to control the amount of steel entering Europe from countries outside the union. To protect local companies in the steel market, a certain quantity of steel can be imported without additional charges, which is known as a tariff quota. If more steel than this quota is imported, the importer must pay an extra charge of 50 percent.

There are two types of these free quotas. The first type is open to all countries that trade with the EU, but the amount is limited based on how much they have previously imported. The second type is special and only applies to countries that have a free trade agreement with the EU. For these countries, there are additional opportunities to import steel without customs duties.

The regulations also state that some countries may receive more favorable conditions than others. For example, Ukraine will be granted a preferential allocation of quotas due to its specific circumstances. The purpose of all these rules is to create order in trade so that companies importing steel know exactly which rules and charges apply to their products.
